en: We must admit that this conception is a very plausible one, because the hypothesis of fixation is based upon the well-known fact, that certain periods of human life, and particularly infancy, do sometimes leave determining traces for ever. The only question is whether this principle is a sufficient explanation or not. If we examine persons who have been neurotic from infancy it seems to be confirmed, for we see the nucleus-complex as a permanent and powerful activity throughout the whole life. But if we take cases which never show any considerable traces of neurosis except at the particular time when they break down, and there are many such, this principle becomes doubtful. If there is such a thing as fixation, it is not permissible to base upon it a new hypothesis, claiming that at times during certain epochs of life the fixation becomes loosened and ineffective, while at others it suddenly becomes strengthened and effective. In such cases we find the nucleus-complex as active and as potent as in those which apparently support the theory of fixation. Here a critical attitude is peculiarly justifiable, when we consider the often-repeated observation that the moment of the outbreak of the disease is by no means indifferent; as a rule it is most critical. It usually occurs at the moment when a new psychological adjustment, that is, a new adaptation, is demanded . Such moments facilitate the outbreak of a neurosis, as every experienced neurologist knows. This fact seems to me extremely significant. If the fixation were indeed real we should expect to find its influence constant, i.e. a neurosis continuous throughout life. This is obviously not the case. The psychological determination of a neurosis is only partially due to an early infantile predisposition; it is due to a certain actual cause as well. And if we carefully examine the kind of infantile phantasies and events to which the neurotic individual is attached, we shall be obliged to agree that there is nothing in them specific for neurosis. Normal individuals have pretty much the same kind of internal and external experiences, and are attached to them to an even astonishing degree, without developing a neurosis. You will find primitive people, especially, very much bound to their infantility. It now begins to look as if this so-called fixation were a normal phenomenon, and that the importance of infancy for the later mental attitude is natural and prevails everywhere. The fact that the neurotic seems to be markedly influenced by his infantile conflicts, shows that it is less a matter of fixation than of a peculiar use which he makes of his infantile past. It looks as if he exaggerated its importance, and attributed a very great artificial value to it (Adler, a pupil of Freud's, expresses a very similar view). It would be unjust to say that Freud confined himself to the hypothesis of fixation; he also was conscious of the impression I have just discussed. He called this phenomenon of reactivation or secondary exaggeration of infantile reminiscences "regression." But in Freud's conception it appears as if the incestuous desires of the Œdipus-complex were the real cause of the regression to infantile phantasies. If this were the case, we should have to postulate an unexpected intensity of the primary incestuous tendencies. This view led Freud to his recent comparison between the so-called psychological "incest-barrier" in children and the "incest-taboo" in primitive man. He supposes that a real incestuous desire has led the primitive man to the invention of a protective law; while to me it looks as if the incest-taboo is one among numerous taboos of all sorts, and due to the typical superstitious fear of primitive man, a fear existing independently of incest and its interdiction. I am able to attribute as little particular strength to incestuous desires in childhood as in primitive humanity. I do not even seek the reason for regression in primary incestuous or any other sexual desires. I must state that a purely sexual ætiology of neurosis seems to me much too narrow. I base this criticism upon no prejudice against sexuality, but upon an intimate acquaintance with the whole problem.
zh: 我們必須承認，這個概念相當合理，因為固著假說是基於一個眾所周知的事實：人類生命的某些時期，特別是嬰兒期，有時確實會留下永久的決定性痕跡。唯一的問題是，這個原則是否足以作為充分的解釋。如果我們檢視那些從嬰兒期就出現神經症的人，這個假說似乎得到證實，因為我們看到核心情結在整個生命中作為一種持久而強大的活動存在。但若我們考慮那些除了在特定崩潰時刻之外從未顯示任何顯著神經症跡象的案例——而且這樣的情況很多——這個原則就變得可疑。如果固著這種現象確實存在，我們不能據此建立一個新假說，聲稱在某些生命階段固著會變得鬆弛無效，而在其他階段卻突然增強並產生作用。在這些案例中，我們發現核心情結的活躍與強度，與那些表面上支持固著理論的案例並無二致。在此，批判態度特別合理，因為我們考慮到一個反覆觀察到的現象：疾病爆發的時機絕非偶然；通常它發生在最關鍵的時刻。它往往發生在需要新的心理調整——即新的適應——的時刻。正如每位經驗豐富的神經科醫師所知，這樣的時刻容易引發神經症。在我看來，這個事實極其重要。如果固著確實存在，我們應該會發現其影響是持續的，即神經症貫穿整個生命。但顯然情況並非如此。神經症的心理決定因素僅部分來自早期嬰兒期的傾向；它同樣也源於某種當下的原因。如果我們仔細檢驗神經症患者所依附的那種嬰兒期幻想與事件，我們將不得不承認，其中並無任何對神經症而言是特異的。正常個體也有大致相同的內在與外在經驗，並且對它們依附到甚至令人驚訝的程度，卻沒有發展出神經症。你會發現原始民族尤其深受其嬰兒性的束縛。現在看來，這種所謂的固著似乎是一種正常現象，而嬰兒期對後來心理態度的影響是自然而普遍的。神經症患者似乎明顯受其嬰兒期衝突影響的事實，顯示這與其說是固著的問題，不如說是他對自己嬰兒期過去的一種特殊運用。看起來他誇大了其重要性，並賦予它一種極大的人為價值（佛洛伊德的學生阿德勒表達了非常相似的觀點）。說佛洛伊德僅限於固著假說是不公平的；他也意識到我剛才討論的印象。他將這種嬰兒期記憶的重新活化或二次誇大現象稱為「退行」。但在佛洛伊德的觀念中，似乎伊底帕斯情結的亂倫慾望才是退行至嬰兒期幻想的真正原因。如果真是如此，我們就必須假定原始亂倫傾向具有出乎意料的強度。這種觀點使佛洛伊德最近將兒童中所謂的心理「亂倫屏障」與原始人中的「亂倫禁忌」進行比較。他假設一種真實的亂倫慾望導致原始人發明瞭一種保護性法律；而在我看來，亂倫禁忌只是眾多各類禁忌中的一種，源於原始人典型的迷信恐懼——一種獨立於亂倫及其禁令而存在的恐懼。我認為，無論是童年還是原始人類，亂倫慾望都沒有什麼特別的強度。我甚至不將退行的原因歸結於原始的亂倫慾望或任何其他性慾望。我必須聲明，純粹性的神經症病因學在我看來過於狹隘。我提出這一批評並非基於對性的偏見，而是基於對整個問題的深入瞭解。
